metadata.summary: Restrict AI-healed selectors to same accessibility role+name and require every heal to land as a reviewable diff before CI consumes it, preventing silent test rot in E2E suites.

content.title: Self-Healing Locators with Mandatory Audit Diff
content.summary: In Playwright, Cypress, or Selenium suites where an AI healer auto-repairs broken selectors, restrict healing to candidates that match the original locator's accessibility role and accessible name, and require every heal to land as a reviewable diff (`healed-selectors.diff` or equivalent) before the next CI run consumes it. Auto-healing without an audit trail is silent test rot; allowing arbitrary CSS-substitution heals is how an E2E suite ends up clicking the wrong button on a payment screen.

why.p: Microsoft's Playwright self-healing benchmark reports ~75% success on selector-related failures via accessibility-tree matching, with maintenance time dropping ~33% in the first quarter; accessibility-tree heals (`Role: button, Name: Checkout`) are roughly 10× more stable than DOM-class heals (`div.checkout-btn-v3`). Healing is therefore high-leverage but high-risk: a wrong heal in an auth or payment flow turns a real bug into a silent green build. The two-part rule — same-role-and-name + human-reviewed diff — is the published mitigation; without it, auto-heal is a foot-gun.

list.item: Large E2E suites (>200 tests) with regular UI churn from a fast-shipping product team.
Design-system migrations and component-rename refactors where DOM changes en masse.
Cypress / Playwright projects already using role-based or test-id locators (`getByRole`, `getByTestId`).
Cross-browser regression suites where flake from selector drift dominates failures.
High-stakes flows: payments, login, role-elevation, account deletion — wrong heal = wrong click = silent prod regression.
Small E2E suites (<50 tests) where humans can repair selectors faster than reviewing heal diffs.
Suites that locate elements by visual position or pixel coordinate — no accessible name to anchor on.
A/B-tested UI where the same role+name appears in two variants — heals collide.

section.name: role-name-anchor
section.p: Microsoft's Playwright self-heal benchmark reports stability ~10× higher when the candidate is matched by accessibility tree (role + name) instead of DOM (tag + class). The mechanism is intuitive: roles and names express user-visible intent; classes express the implementation that just changed.

rule.statement: The healer MUST extract the original locator's accessibility role AND accessible name (case-insensitive, whitespace-normalized regex). A heal candidate is acceptable ONLY when its role equals the original role AND its accessible name matches the original name pattern. Heals based on tag, CSS class, XPath structural index, or text-only matching are forbidden and MUST be rejected before being written to the diff.
rule.rationale: Role+name = "what the user thinks the element is"; class/structure = "how the framework rendered it last week." Drift in the latter is ubiquitous and harmless; drift in the former is a real change that should fail the test, not be auto-healed.

example.note: Class changed from `.cta-v3` to `.cta-v4`; role + name preserved; safe heal.

example.code: // healer candidate rejected: same class but role changed // Checkout -> REJECT (role link != button)
example.note: The page replaced a button with a link; tests should fail loudly, not heal silently.

antipattern.description: Healer falls back to "first interactive element with matching text" when role+name fails.
antipattern.reason: That fallback heals a "Cancel" button onto a page where the team renamed Cancel to "Discard"; the original "Cancel" elsewhere on the page (a different action) gets the click and silent prod incidents follow.

section.name: audit-diff-gate
section.p: The same property that makes a healer useful — running between failing CI run and retry — also makes it dangerous. The mitigation is a one-way valve: heals are written to a diff file; the diff is committed and reviewed; only after review does the healed selector replace the test source.

rule.statement: When the healer accepts a candidate, it MUST write the change as a unified diff to `healed-selectors.diff` (committed to the repo) and EXIT NONZERO so the CI run fails. A separate `healer-apply` job MUST refuse to run unless `healed-selectors.diff` carries an approving review marker (e.g., commit message tag `healer:approved` or a YAML side-car). High-stakes flow files (payments, auth) listed in `healer-policy.json` MUST be excluded from auto-heal entirely.
rule.rationale: Without the diff gate, a wrong heal becomes the new ground truth on the next CI run; with the gate, every heal is a code-review event the team sees and can reject.

example.code: CI run #481: test failed -> healer found candidate role=button name=/Check ?out/i wrote healed-selectors.diff (1 file, 1 line) exit 1 (build red, PR blocked) Reviewer approves diff, merges. CI run #482: healer-apply consumes diff (commit tag healer:approved present) tests pass
example.note: One-way valve: heal then review, never silent.

example.code: CI run #481: test failed -> healer found candidate, rewrote test in place, commit pushed to main, build green.
example.note: Wrong heal lives in main forever, no human ever saw the diff.

antipattern.description: "Auto-merge healer PRs" rule whitelists the bot for all PRs touching `tests/`.
antipattern.reason: Defeats the gate; a wrong heal slips through with the same opacity as silent in-place rewriting.

template.purpose: Policy file the healer reads: allowed roles, forbidden flows, diff path.
CI workflow snippet that fails when healed-selectors.diff exists without review.
